title: Ancient DNA Extraction from Dental Calculus
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"fkf36","text":"Silica-based total DNA extraction protocol optimised for the recovery of ultra-short DNA molecules from archaeological dental calculus, modified from Dabney et al. (2013) PNAS (doi: 10.1073/pnas.1314445110) and adapted for dental calculus by Mann et al. (2018) Scientific Reports (doi: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-28091-9). ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":157,"length":6},{"style":"italic","offset":171,"length":4},{"style":"italic","offset":247,"length":6},{"style":"italic","offset":261,"length":19}],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":182,"length":23},{"key":1,"offset":286,"length":42}],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"DEE625306A8911EAB1C8E91D5E68E95D","url":"https://dx.doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1314445110"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"2E756D83C1DF11EA9D1E0A58A9FEAC2A","url":"https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-28091-9"}}}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"971dq","text":"Working in an Ancient DNA Laboratory","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":36}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"cm19i","text":"- All steps of the protocol should take place in a clean room facility specifically designed for ancient DNA.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"41mav","text":"- The researcher performing lab work should wear correspondingly suitable lab-wear, such as:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4s0av","text":"   - full-body suit with hood (e.g., Tyvek)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fav5l","text":"   - hairnet","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"eqs8c","text":"   - face mask","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5qtoi","text":"   - two pairs of clean gloves (do not wear latex gloves if samples will be used for proteomic analyses)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"o3b4","text":"   - clean shoes ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6fkjq","text":"   - protective glasses","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"483so","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"dll58","text":"- Sample processing should be carried out in separated work benches with integrated UV irradiation (e.g. Dead Air PCR work bench)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"a0ouu","text":"- Surfaces and equipment should be regularly decontaminated with e.g. bleach solution or Thermofisher's DNA AWAY (or similar) and irradiated with UV.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"84qk","text":"- All home-made buffers should be prepared in a separate, dedicated PCR-free ultra-clean room and UV-irradiated for 30 min.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"dvr5f","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"brls8","text":"Please see the following for more detailed guidance: ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"a306u","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2cp46","text":"Llamas, B. et al., 2017. From the field to the laboratory: Controlling DNA contamination in human ancient DNA research in the high-throughput sequencing era. STAR: Science \u0026 Technology of Archaeological Research, 3(1), pp.1–14. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1080/20548923.2016.1258824.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":158,"length":53}],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":242,"length":45}],"data":[]},{"key":"2a82v","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"cjtuh","text":"Definitions","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"rnmi","text":"Stock-aliquot refers to a personal 'stock' (e.g. in a 50ml Falcon Tube) of reagents you can use across multiple sessions of this protocol. An 'aliquot' refers to a sub-aliquot of the stock, that is used for a single session of this specific protocol.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6k24p","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2inim","text":"Protocol Specific Guidelines","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":28}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"bt7td","text":"This protocol requires the use of two rooms - a dedicated PCR-free ultra-clean buffer preparation room and a DNA extraction room. As calculus typically contains higher biomass than bones or teeth, it is preferable to do calculus extractions in a separate space when one is available. This reduces the possibility of contaminating extractions from lower biomass samples with oral bacterial DNA. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ad23l","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1q4bg","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"","data":{"guid":"727FE9E06A8D11EAB1C8E91D5E68E95D","url":"https://doi.org/10.1080/20548923.2016.1258824"}}}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"75hie","text":"Planning","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"72us1","text":"This protocol takes four days, out of which three include laboratory work and one is an incubation period.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3436m","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"eas7s","text":"Only the extraction buffer can be prepared within the DNA extraction room. All other home-made buffers must be prepared in a separate dedicated PCR-free ultra-clean room, and we typically UV-irradiate these for 30 min. Purchased kits should be DNA-free.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"b6060","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6s217","text":"Check waste disposal guidance for all reagents in this protocol against your corresponding laboratory regulations.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5egug","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fhsvm","text":"Equipment","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":9}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8nll2","text":"Make sure all necessary equipment is available (see Materials).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"d0gjp","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"57kk","text":"Abbreviations","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":13}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fb3vt","text":"EDTA = Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5gb2a","text":"GuHCl = Guanidinium chloride or Guanidine hydrochloride","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3du9p","text":"HPLC = High Performance Liquid Chromatography (-Grade Water)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"d65nb","text":"NaClO = Sodium hypochlorite","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"bu1fj","text":"TE = Tris-EDTA","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"bc8c0","text":"TET = Tris-EDTA-Tween (-buffer)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"a1dba","text":"UV = Ultraviolet (radiation)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"all2i","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5f9a2","text":"Samples","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6krr5","text":"Ensure sample aliquots (2-5mg) of dental calculus (in 2ml tubes) are prepared in a dedicated sampling room, prior to the day you start this protocol.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"bc0c9","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2fbjk","text":"Controls","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4vr3v","text":"Take along a positive control (sample of known performance) and a negative control (empty tube) in order to assess the performance of the protocol and the level of background contamination. Consider these two extra samples in your calculations for buffer preparations. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"fluf5","text":"Reagents","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7nj7h","text":"Household bleach solution (2-6%) diluted to a working concentration of 0.2-0.5 % NaClO in total","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":33}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1ts66","text":"- H290 May be corrosive to metals.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"emf1f","text":"- H314 Causes severe skin burns and eye damage.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1fd3m","text":"- H411 Toxic to aquatic life with long lasting effects.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2sj2b","text":"- EUH206 Warning! Do not use together with other products. May release dangerous gases (chlorine). Remove from surface after recommended incubation time with water-soaked tissue.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"yqm6i","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"yrb4r","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":1,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"fn1on","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":null,"entityRanges":null,"data":{}},{"key":"eogll","text":"DNA AWAY","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"5ol14","text":"- H314 Causes severe skin burns and eye damage.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"xz7yo","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":2,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"hp27","text":"Note: Both bleach solutions and DNA AWAY are used for decontamintation. DNA AWAY is less corrosive than bleach and should be preferred for decontamination of sensitive equipments such as surfaces of electric devices.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2fb0p","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2486h","text":"GuHCl","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"62pmc","text":"- steps: Prepare cleaned workspace with all necessary reagents and equipment. If lab-wide large-batch pre-prepared reagent stores are used, ensure to have made personal stock-aliquot of reagents such as UV-Water, EDTA, sodium acetate, and proteinase K in amounts sufficient for this extraction.
Prepare binding buffer calculating 10 mL, / reaction . %s %s

Weigh GuHCl and transfer into a glass bottle. If you want to clean the area where GuHCl was used, first use water and then bleach solution. Do not use bleach directly as it reacts with GuHCl to produce toxic chlorine gas!
Add UV-irradiated HPLC water (6 mL). This reaction is endothermic and the tube will become very cold. Be aware of the unusual 'slushy' way of dissolving.

Gently shake horizontally in order to get the salt dissolved. If necessary, apply short ( 00:00:10 ) bursts in microwave (~400W) keeping the tube slightly unscrewed. Wait until bottle cools down between microwave bursts.
Pipette isopropanol (4 mL) to reach the final reaction volume ( 10 mL ).
Prepare wash buffer by pipetting 40 mL ethanol to the wash buffer from the High Pure Viral Nucleic Acid kit following manufacturer's instuctions and make an aliquot calculating 900 µL, / reaction .

Prepare TET elution buffer by making an aliquot of TE-buffer calculating 100 µL, / reaction and pipette Tween-20 accordingly to reach 0.05 % (v/v) concentration to make 'TET'. Because Tween-20 is highly viscous, we dilute it 1:10 in UV-HPLC water, and use this 10% dilution to add Tween-20 to the TE-buffer
Irradiate all buffers with UV for 00:30:00 without the lids. UV irradiation can be done together with solutions from steps 1 (binding buffer), 4 (wash buffer), and 5 (TET buffer).

Store binding buffer in a fridge at 4 °C overnight for day 4. Label the bottle accordingly with the name, date and for the calculated amount of reactions. Buffer can be stored in a fridge for up to four weeks. Seal bottle with parafilm to avoid evaporation.
Dilute proteinase K powder ( 100 mg ) in 10 mL UV HPLC-water to a working concentration of 10 mg / 1 mL . Store proteinase K solution at -20 °C for use on day 2.

UV sterilize calculus. Irradiate samples for 00:01:00 , then shake the samples and irradiate for another 00:01:00 . Aim for 2-5 mg starting material. If the sample is in powder-form, irradiate in the original tube with the lid open. If the sample is in large pieces, place calculus in an aluminium foil boat in the crosslinker to maximize exposure.
Add 1 mL of fresh 0.5 M EDTA to each sample pellet.

Seal tubes with Parafilm, rotate Overnight, (12-18h) with low overhead rotation speed (e.g., 12-16 rpm) at room temperature. If the tubes will be exposed to UV irridation, such as whole-room irridation for sterilization, be sure to cover them with foil or otherwise shield them. Post-incubation sample suspended in EDTA can safely be stored in a freezer ( -20 °C ) before isolation and clean-up.

For each reaction prepare one 50 mL Falcon tube, one High Pure Extender Assembly (i.e. Falcon tube from kit containing funnel and purification column), two collection tubes from the kit, and one 1.5 mL LoBind tube for final elution step.

In every 50 mL Falcon tube pipette 10 mL binding buffer and 400 µL sodium acetate (UV-irradiated). Mix by inversion and measure pH (should be 5-6). Add more sodium acetate if the pH is too high. If the pH is too low you can add sodium hydroxide.
Remove parafilm from extraction tubes, then spin the tubes for 00:02:00 at 18500 x g, °C, to pellet calculus. If pellet is not solid, repeat centrifugation.
Pipette supernatant to respective 50 mL Falcon tube, mix contents by inversion. If pellet is too fragile, repeat centrifugation before transferring supernatant. Store the calculus pellet at -20 °C .
Pipette binding buffer/extract mix to High Pure Extender Assembly.

Spin at a maximum of 1500 rpm, °C, for 00:08:00 This RPM is specific to a 50 mL Thermo Scientific TX-400 Swinging Bucket Rotor. As this is a swing rotor, the rpm value maybe inconsistent for other models. Therefore this value must be adjusted on a per-machine basis. Convert the rpm to rcf (g) and determine the appropriate rpm for your instrument. You can also turn the tube 180 o after 00:04:00 to ensure the liquid does not get stuck on the inner rim of the funnel During this centrifugation step, we recommend preparing downstream steps, such as labelling of final elution tubes.

Pipette any liquids remaining in the funnel onto the column. Remove funnel from column and insert the column into a fresh 2 mL collection tube. The flow-through stays in the collection tube of the 50mL High Pure Extender Assembly and can be discarded.
Dry spin the column in the collection tube for 00:02:00 at 18500 x g, °C, .
Pipette 450 µL wash buffer from the High Pure Viral Nucleic Acid kit to the column, and spin at 8000 x g, °C, for 00:01:00 .
Remove column from the collection tube, discard the flow-through and the old collection tube, and put the column into a fresh 2 mL collection tube.

Repeat washing step once ( go to step #24 ) reusing the collection tube, and discard flow-through. Discard flow-through in one of two following ways: Remove all liquid in the collection tube with a pipette, or Pour off the liquid into a fresh waste tube, and pat the rim of the collection tube dry on a paper tissue or towl. Use just one spot on the paper tissue per sample. Be careful not to touch the rim of the tube on the waste container. Be sure to clean the surface with DNA Away or bleach after discarding the paper.

Dry spin at 18500 x g, °C, for 00:01:00 To ensure the liquid does not get stuck on the inner rim of the funnel, you can optionally spin for 00:00:30 , turn the tube 180 o , and dry spin for another 00:00:30

Transfer column to a fresh 1.5 mL LoBind Tube, pipette 50 µL of Tris-EDTA-tween (TET) to the center of column, incubate for 00:03:00 on the benchtop, and spin 00:01:00 at 18500 x g, °C, to elute the DNA. Eluted DNA will be stored in this tube. Label tube on top and side accordingly.
Repeat elution step for a total elution volume of 100 µL TET.
Store the DNA extracts at -20 °C .

If the calculus was in a boat for irradiation place it back in a 1.5 mL or 2 mL tube. Add 1 mL of 0.5 M EDTA to each sample, to remove surface contaminants. This step can be skipped if the purpose of the study is to detect infectuous pathogens, as these are more likely to be located on the outer layer of the calculus deposit (however, this may lead to a higher proportion of environmental contaminants in the data).

Vortex and incubate on rotator at room temperature for 00:15:00 . The incubation step can lead to some sample loss, as the calculus will start dissolving and releasing DNA. If the calculus is very powdery, the supernatant can be removed directly after vortexing, without any incubation. Otherwise the calculus solubilizes within the 15 min and you lose DNA while removing the EDTA.
Spin tubes 00:02:00 at 18500 x g, °C, .
Transfer supernatant to a separate tube, labelled "EDTA-wash". Store the "EDTA-wash" tube at -20 °C for potential future analyses.

Remove Parafilm and make sure that the tubes have not leaked (if they have leaked, clean them). The appearance of the calculus will vary depending on its initial state. Larger pieces will have barely changed at all, while powder will be almost gone.
Briefly centrifuge tubes to remove any liquid from the lids.
Add 50 µL of 10 mg/ml proteinase K to each tube.

Vortex tubes, seal with Parafilm, and continue decalcification by rotating at low overhead rotation speed (e.g., 12-16 rpm) at room temperature for 24-48 hours. After starting the incubation, we recommend beginning preparations for day 4, such as pre-labelling the falcon tubes that will be used in step 17. Decalcification time can be adapted, so that the sample is completely decalcified before starting DNA isolation. After complete decalcification, the sample should appear buoyant and feathery, or disappear altogether. Post-incubation suspension can safely be stored in a freezer ( -20 °C ) before isolation and clean-up.
Prepare cleaned workspace with all necessary reagents and equipment.
creator: Zandra Fagernäs
